cases.prompt:
Title: The Eiffel Tower

The Eiffel Tower is a wrought-iron lattice tower on the Champ de Mars in Paris, France. It is named after the engineer Gustave Eiffel, whose company designed and built the tower between 1887 and 1889 as the centerpiece of the 1889 World's Fair. Standing 330 metres tall, it was the tallest man-made structure in the world for 41 years until the completion of the Chrysler Building in New York in 1930. The tower receives almost seven million visitors a year, making it the most-visited paid monument in the world.
cases.source: The Eiffel Tower is a wrought-iron lattice tower on the Champ de Mars in Paris, France. It is named after the engineer Gustave Eiffel, whose company designed and built the tower between 1887 and 1889 as the centerpiece of the 1889 World's Fair. Standing 330 metres tall, it was the tallest man-made structure in the world for 41 years until the completion of the Chrysler Building in New York in 1930. The tower receives almost seven million visitors a year, making it the most-visited paid monument in the world.

cases.prompt:
Title: How Photosynthesis Works

Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy stored in glucose. Inside chloroplasts, the pigment chlorophyll absorbs sunlight, which drives a reaction that splits water molecules and combines carbon dioxide from the air into sugars, releasing oxygen as a by-product. The light-dependent reactions capture energy as ATP and NADPH, while the Calvin cycle uses that energy to build glucose. Because it produces the oxygen most organisms breathe and forms the base of nearly every food chain, photosynthesis underpins life on Earth.
cases.source: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy stored in glucose. Inside chloroplasts, the pigment chlorophyll absorbs sunlight, which drives a reaction that splits water molecules and combines carbon dioxide from the air into sugars, releasing oxygen as a by-product. The light-dependent reactions capture energy as ATP and NADPH, while the Calvin cycle uses that energy to build glucose. Because it produces the oxygen most organisms breathe and forms the base of nearly every food chain, photosynthesis underpins life on Earth.

cases.prompt:
Title: Markets Edge Higher as Inflation Cools

Global equity markets rose modestly on Tuesday as investors weighed cooler than expected inflation data against cautious signals from central banks. The benchmark index closed up 0.6 percent, led by technology shares, while energy stocks lagged amid softer crude oil prices. Bond yields slipped as traders increased bets on an interest-rate cut later in the year. Analysts warned that volatility could return ahead of next week's central bank meeting, where policymakers are expected to signal the path of future rates.
cases.source: Global equity markets rose modestly on Tuesday as investors weighed cooler than expected inflation data against cautious signals from central banks. The benchmark index closed up 0.6 percent, led by technology shares, while energy stocks lagged amid softer crude oil prices. Bond yields slipped as traders increased bets on an interest-rate cut later in the year. Analysts warned that volatility could return ahead of next week's central bank meeting, where policymakers are expected to signal the path of future rates.

cases.prompt:
Title: What Is a Black Hole?

A black hole is a region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing — not even light — can escape once it crosses the boundary known as the event horizon. Black holes form when massive stars collapse at the end of their lives, compressing enormous mass into an extremely small volume. Although they cannot be seen directly, astronomers detect them by observing the intense radiation emitted by gas and dust as it spirals inward, and by the motion of nearby stars. In 2019, the Event Horizon Telescope captured the first image of a black hole's shadow, in the galaxy M87.
cases.source: A black hole is a region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing — not even light — can escape once it crosses the boundary known as the event horizon. Black holes form when massive stars collapse at the end of their lives, compressing enormous mass into an extremely small volume. Although they cannot be seen directly, astronomers detect them by observing the intense radiation emitted by gas and dust as it spirals inward, and by the motion of nearby stars. In 2019, the Event Horizon Telescope captured the first image of a black hole's shadow, in the galaxy M87.

cases.prompt:
Title: A Brief History of Coffee

Coffee originated in the highlands of Ethiopia, where, according to legend, a goatherd named Kaldi noticed his goats becoming energetic after eating the berries of a certain shrub. By the 15th century, coffee was being cultivated and traded in Yemen, and it spread through the Arabian Peninsula, where coffee houses became centers of conversation and commerce. Over the following centuries coffee reached Europe, the Americas, and beyond, becoming one of the most widely consumed beverages in the world. Today it is a global commodity supporting the livelihoods of millions of farmers.
cases.source: Coffee originated in the highlands of Ethiopia, where, according to legend, a goatherd named Kaldi noticed his goats becoming energetic after eating the berries of a certain shrub. By the 15th century, coffee was being cultivated and traded in Yemen, and it spread through the Arabian Peninsula, where coffee houses became centers of conversation and commerce. Over the following centuries coffee reached Europe, the Americas, and beyond, becoming one of the most widely consumed beverages in the world. Today it is a global commodity supporting the livelihoods of millions of farmers.

cases.prompt:
Title: Apollo 11

Apollo 11 was the spaceflight that first landed humans on the Moon. Commander Neil Armstrong and lunar module pilot Buzz Aldrin landed the Apollo Lunar Module Eagle on July 20, 1969, at 20:17 UTC, while Michael Collins orbited above in the command module Columbia. Armstrong became the first person to step onto the lunar surface six hours and 39 minutes later, on July 21. They spent about two and a quarter hours outside the spacecraft and collected 47.5 pounds of lunar material to bring back to Earth.
cases.source: Apollo 11 was the spaceflight that first landed humans on the Moon. Commander Neil Armstrong and lunar module pilot Buzz Aldrin landed the Apollo Lunar Module Eagle on July 20, 1969, at 20:17 UTC, while Michael Collins orbited above in the command module Columbia. Armstrong became the first person to step onto the lunar surface six hours and 39 minutes later, on July 21. They spent about two and a quarter hours outside the spacecraft and collected 47.5 pounds of lunar material to bring back to Earth.

output.text: Photosynthesis is the process that allows green plants, algae, and some bacteria to convert light energy into chemical energy in the form of glucose. This occurs in chloroplasts, where chlorophyll absorbs sunlight, splitting water molecules and combining carbon dioxide to produce sugars while releasing oxygen. The light-dependent reactions generate energy in the form of ATP and NADPH, which is then utilized in the Calvin cycle to synthesize glucose. This process is essential for producing oxygen and serves as the foundation for nearly all food chains, making it vital for life on Earth.
